title: Synthesizing Test-Driven Development
concept: Wiring the agent to write a test first, verify failure, write implementation, and verify success.

exercises.prompt: What should you avoid when developing with coding agents?
exercises.explain: Ignoring security alerts can lead to vulnerabilities and problems in your code. Be sure to pay attention!

cards.title: What is Test-Driven Development?
cards.body: Test-Driven Development (TDD) is a coding approach where you write tests before writing the actual code. This helps ensure your code works correctly from the start!

cards.title: What Does TDD Mean?
cards.body: TDD stands for Test-Driven Development. In TDD, you first create a test for your code's function, run it, and then write the code to pass that test.

cards.title: TDD is Like Building a House
cards.body: Imagine building a house. First, you draw the plans (the tests), and then you build according to those plans (the code). If the plans are wrong, you fix them before you build!

cards.title: Example of TDD in Action
cards.body: Suppose we want to create a function that adds two numbers. We start by writing a test to check if 1 + 1 equals 2, then we implement the function to make the test pass.

cards.title: Myth: TDD is Wasteful
cards.body: Some believe TDD takes too much time, but it actually saves time by catching bugs early and reducing future headaches!

cards.title: The TDD Process
cards.body: The TDD process involves these steps: write a failing test, write code to make it pass, and then refactor the code.

right.text: 2. Write code to make the test pass.

exercises.prompt: What is the first step in Test-Driven Development?

options.text: Write a test that will fail

exercises.explain: The first step in TDD is writing a test that will fail. This helps you set a goal for your implementation.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In TDD, you should always write your tests after you write your code.
exercises.explain: False! In Test-Driven Development, you write your tests before you write the actual code.

exercises.prompt: When using TDD, always start by writing a __________ that is expected to fail.
exercises.sentence: When using TDD, always start by writing a test that is expected to fail.
exercises.explain: Starting with a 'test' helps you know what the implementation needs to achieve.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following methods supports Test-Driven Development by incorporating coding best practices?
exercises.explain: Writing tests before implementation supports TDD by ensuring that the written code meets the defined requirements right from the start.

exercises.prompt: What is the correct sequence of steps in Test-Driven Development?

options.text: Write code, run tests, fix bugs
Write tests, write code, verify success
Run existing tests, write new tests

exercises.explain: The correct sequence in Test-Driven Development is to first write tests, then write the code to pass those tests, and finally verify that everything works. This approach leads to cleaner and more efficient code!
